coredata.dc:title: MOF and CdTe quantum dots entrapped hydrogel film as dual-signal ratiometric fluorometric-colorimetric sensing platform for erythromycin detection: Portable smartphone-assisted visual sensing in milk and water samples
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Azeez, Kurdistan Fakhraldin
Salimi, Abdollah
Hallaj, Rahman
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we have established a portable, sensitive, and efficient method for the detection of erythromycin (ERY) in serum, as well as in food samples, including milk and water, which present a significant challenge. Herein, we have designed a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel film platform that facilitates a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetry sensing system using an aluminum metal-organic framework (Al-MOF) with the emission wavelength of 418 nm and CdTe QDs at the emission wavelength of 528 nm as fluorophores. The variation of the I418/I528 ratio versus ERY concentration was linear at a concentration range of up to 242 nM and 728 pM with a detection limit of 2.16 nM and 2.42 pM in liquid state and hydrogel film, respectively. This solid-state assay indicates a 500-fold improvement in sensitivity compared to the liquid state and also strong selectivity observed for ERY over other drugs such as amoxicillin, ceftazidime, and so forth. Furthermore, a visual sensing platform supported by smartphones was developed to accurately determine ERY by identifying the precise variations in RGB values. Additionally, this assay was effectively used to detect ERY in serum, tap water, and milk samples with exceptional sensitivity and accuracy in both liquid and hydrogel forms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Erythromycin
Aluminum-metal organic framework
Quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence assay
Hydrogel film state
Dual-mode detection
Visual sensing
Smartphone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe/QDs)
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ERY in liquid and hydrogel state with QDs@Al-MOF as a probe
Smartphone application used to identify and visualizing ERY

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number P-S-28-12-2022 ) and Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of Al-MOF. (B) TEM image of QDs. (C) Different excitation of Al-MOF. (D) Different excitation of QDs (E) FT-IR spectra of NH2-BDC and Al-MOF (F) XRD of Al-MOF.

: (A) Different excitation of QDs@Al-MOF in liquid state (B) Different excitation of QDs@Al-MOF in hydrogel film state (C) 2D excitation of QDs@Al-MOF. (D, E) Fluorescence emission spectra of QDs, Al-MOF and QDs@Al-MOF in liquid and hydrogel film state. (F) Digital photographs of QDs@Al-MOF-PVA, ERY-QDs@Al-MOF-PVA and PVA under visible (left side) and UV light (right side).

Fluorescence spectrum of QDs@Al-MOF with increasing ERY (A) Concentrations (24.8 to 242.6 nM) in liquid state (B) Concentration (26 to 728pM) in hydrogel film-state. (C) The calibration depicting the relationship between I418/I528 and ERY concentration is accompanied by (an inset image that displays the corresponding fluorescence picture captured under a 365 nm UV lamp) in liquid state. (D) Calibration curve of hydrogel film-state QDs@Al-MOF with the increasing of ERY concentration. (E, F) Time effect the stability of ERY-QDs@Al-MOF for hydrogel film and liquid states.

(A) Fluorimetry based on smartphone-assisted visual test (B) The correlation between the G/mean(R + G + B) values of the liquid state and the concentration of ERY is demonstrated. The inset exhibits fluorescence images captured under a 365 nm UV light, showcasing the progression of ERY concentrations up to 242.6 nM (C,D) Upon optimizing the conditions, the fluorescence response of QDs@Al-MOF was evaluated to assess the selectivity of the method in both hydrogel film states and liquid.
Comparison of different methods for ERY analysis.

row.entry:
24.8 to 242.6 nM
in liquid state
26 to 728 pM
(in hydrogel film state)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determination and recovery test conducted on ERY spiked in a real sample.

head.ce:title: MOF and CdTe quantum dots entrapped hydrogel film as dual-signal ratiometric fluorometric-colorimetric sensing platform for erythromycin detection: Portable smartphone-assisted visual sensing in milk and water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Koya KOY45, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Health, Koya University, Koya KOY45, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175 Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we have established a portable, sensitive, and efficient method for the detection of erythromycin (ERY) in serum, as well as in food samples, including milk and water, which present a significant challenge. Herein, we have designed a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) hydrogel film platform that facilitates a dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence/colorimetry sensing system using an aluminum metal-organic framework (Al-MOF) with the emission wavelength of 418 nm and CdTe QDs at the emission wavelength of 528 nm as fluorophores. The variation of the I418/I528 ratio versus ERY concentration was linear at a concentration range of up to 242 nM and 728 pM with a detection limit of 2.16 nM and 2.42 pM in liquid state and hydrogel film, respectively. This solid-state assay indicates a 500-fold improvement in sensitivity compared to the liquid state and also strong selectivity observed for ERY over other drugs such as amoxicillin, ceftazidime, and so forth. Furthermore, a visual sensing platform supported by smartphones was developed to accurately determine ERY by identifying the precise variations in RGB values. Additionally, this assay was effectively used to detect ERY in serum, tap water, and milk samples with exceptional sensitivity and accuracy in both liquid and hydrogel forms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since medicines can enhance the emergence and evolution of antibiotic-resistant strains, their presence in the environment has become a serious concern for ecological health as well as public health (Feng et al., 2023). Erythromycin is a macrolide antibiotic with a broad spectrum of action and is commonly used to treat a variety of bacterial infections, such as those affecting the skin, soft tissues, urogenital system, and respiratory tract (N.-N. Li et al., 2021; Pawar et al., 2021). Because of its stability, erythromycin degrades too slowly naturally, causing ERY to progressively build up in water sources (Alvarez-Elcoro & Enzler, 1999), which increases the potential threats to human health and the ecology by causing bacteria to become resistant to antibiotics (Du et al., 2021). So, different quantitative techniques for the detection of ERY have been established, including molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) (Ayankojo et al., 2020), potentiometric method (Almehizia et al., 2023), ultrahigh-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (Wang, et al., 2024), and spectroscopic techniques (Shahabadi & Razlansari, 2022) etc. While these traditional detection techniques are very accurate at identifying ERY, they still have some drawbacks for the detection process, like long reaction time, complex systems, a high cost, and additional limitations on the analysis process. Therefore, the development of a new, easy-to-use, inexpensive, and highly sensitive ERY detection technique is especially important. In contrast to the conventional detection techniques described above, fluorescence probes with excellent sensitivity and recyclability are highly considered viable solutions for pesticide and antibiotic detection because they outperform other possibilities (Bahari et al., 2021; Geng et al., 2023; G.-D. Wang et al., 2021). However, the majority of the fluorescent sensing devices that were reported for the detection of antibiotics depended on variations such as pH and probe concentration in a single-signal fluorescence mode (An et al., 2022). To get over this limitation and decrease the effect of analyte-independent parameters, the ratiometric fluorescence techniques as an intrinsic self-calibration method were developed (Hamd-Ghadareh et al., 2019; C. Liu et al., 2020). On the other hand, dual-mode detection exhibits greater reliability in comparison to the single-mode detection approach. Because dual mode reduces outside interference, it increases the accuracy of the results and makes it easier to validate study findings. (Hamd-Ghadareh et al., 2023; Tong et al., 2023). Examples of dual-mode techniques include fluorescence Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (M. Li et al., 2020), fluorescence-electrochemistry (Yang, et al., 2024), fluorescence-colorimetric(Lian et al., 2024; M. Liu et al., 2024; Quan et al., 2024; Shen et al., 2023), and other combinations. Because naked eyes are not very good at detecting minor color changes, fluorescence-colorimetric (dual-mode) technology is especially important for the visual detection of objects (Fan et al., 2023). Fluorimetry based on smartphone-assisted visual tests has been gradually established in recent times. As a result, combining ratiometric fluorescence with smartphone technology offers a fresh and effective method for quickly identifying targets (Azeez et al., 2025; Swain et al., 2023). In addition, various fluorophores have been utilized to detect antibiotics. In particular, the fluorescence sensors that have received the most attention and are commercially available include organic molecules, carbon dots, and luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs). Metal-organic framework (MOF) is a novel class of nanomaterial developed through the coordination of metal ions with organic ligands(Mohtasham et al., 2024). This framework is noted for its low cost, ease of modification, easy synthesis, and wide range of captivating properties. Due to advantages such as simple synthesis, MOFs and their derivatives have been used in numerous fields, including gas storage and separation (Kalago et al., 2024; Xiang et al., 2024), catalysis (Lu et al., 2024), drug delivery (Wang, et al., 2024), smart sensing (Yang, et al., 2024; Zou et al., 2021), and bioimaging (Nguyen et al., 2024). The possibility of producing MOFs with multiple luminous emissions (ML-MOFs) is provided by the wide range of metal nodes and ligands (Yin & Yin, 2020).

Quantum dots (QDs) are a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial with a wide range of distinctive optical properties. These include a notable Stokes shift, a broad absorption spectrum, a considerable molar extinction coefficient, an exact and symmetrical fluorescence spectrum, extraordinary photochemical stability, and a surprisingly high quantum yield (Ma et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2020). However, QDs have significantly influenced biological applications, particularly in the fields of biosensing and bioimaging (Sudewi et al., 2024; Vijeata et al., 2024; Wegner & Hildebrandt, 2024). Also, in recent years, ratiometric fluorescent systems based on QDs and MOFs have been recognized as optimal optical sensors for the identification of a range of analytes (Chi et al., 2024; Li, et al., 2024).

This study developed a novel approach for detecting ERY in both liquid and hydrogel film states using fluorescence and colorimetry as an integrated dual-mode sensing assay. To improve sensitivity and selectivity, we trapped the CdTe QDs and Al-MOF fluorophore in a hydrogel film, as shown in Scheme 1 . The Al-MOF was synthesized by a single-step hydrothermal process employing aluminum ions as the central metal ions and NH2-BDC as the ligand. The fluorescence intensity of the CdTe QDs (I528) decreased, while that of the Al-MOF (I418) increased in the presence of ERY. Similarly, the fluorescence color change under a 365 nm UV lamp also showed the alteration of (I418/I528), enabling visual detection of ERY using a smartphone's color software. Furthermore, the entrapment of the suggested fluorophore in hydrogel film not only improved the sensitivity and selectivity of the assay but also enhanced the preparation of the flexible sensing platform. The proposed ratiometric sensor successfully detected ERY concentrations in different samples, such as human serum, milk, and water, in both liquid and hydrogel film states with satisfactory results.

All reagents in this experiment were analytical grade and used directly without further purification. Aluminum (III) chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O), 2-Aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC), Thioglycolic acid (TGA,99 %), Urea, Dimethylformamide (DMF), Methanol, Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4,96 %), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), Cadmium chloride. Pentahydrate (CdCl2.5H2O, 99 %), Tellurium powder (99.9 %), (Sigma Aldrich). Erythromycin, amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, ceftazidime, clindamycin, penicillin, metronidazole and sulfamethoxazole, distilled water with deionization was utilized.

The fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer and a SPECTROD 250-analytikjena spectrophotometer (Germany) respectively, both at room temperature. The scanning electron microscope (SEM) utilized for characterizing the morphology of the product was the MIRA3 TESCAN HV: 20.0 KV from Czech Republic. Additionally, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on a Vector-22 BRUKER spectrophotometer (Switzerland).

The synthesis of Al-MOF was carried out using the hydrothermal method with a specific alteration (C. Li et al., 2019).Initially, 0.724 g of AlCl₃·6H₂O was dissolved in 15 mL of ultrapure water, followed by the addition of 0.544 g of NH₂-BDC with continuous stirring. Subsequently, 0.288 g of urea in a 5 mL aqueous solution was introduced into the mixture and stirred for an additional 30 min. The resulting mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 150 °C for 5 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, the yellow precipitates were separated through centrifugation and washed with ultrapure water. The products were then dried in an oven at 50 °C overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe/QDs)
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe/QDs were prepared according to a published method (Hallaj et al., 2019). In a standard trial, 0.0912 g of CdCl2.5 H2O and 60 μL of TGA were combined with 20 mL of water while stirring. The pH of the mixture was raised to 11.3 by slowly adding 1.0 M NaOH. Simultaneously, 0.0063 g of Te powder and 0.02 g of NaBH4 were precisely measured and added to 1 mL of deionized water in a small flask. A clear yellow CdTe QDs solution was created by introducing the freshly prepared purple NaHTe solution to the earlier prepared Cd mixture under vigorous stirring. Next, the solution underwent reflux at 90 °C for 50 min resulting in a small CdTe cluster solution.

A reported procedure was followed in the preparation of the fluorescent hydrogels (Hamd-Ghadareh et al., 2022). First, 20 mL of water was mixed with 2.0 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), and the mixture was stirred for an hour to create a homogeneous mixture. Fluorescent hydrogels were then created by adding 100 μM of QDs@Al-MOF to the hydrogel and then stirring it for two hours. Lastly, different ERY antibiotic concentrations were added to the hydrogel and stirred for half an hour. The same method was used to produce the PVA hydrogel without QDs@Al-MOF for comparative purposes.

It was established that a 100 μM concentration of QDs@Al-MOF aqueous solution would react with standard ERY solutions in this method. In a typical experiment, the fluorescence intensity of the resultant Al-MOF and QD mixture was measured at wavelengths of 418 nm and 528 nm after excitation at 330 nm. A range of compounds, such as amoxicillin, ciprofloxacin, ceftazidime, clindamycin, penicillin, metronidazole, and sulfamethoxazole, were selected to test the selectivity of ERY to assess the sensing system selectivity.

Milk, human serum, and tap water have been used as real samples. For milk analysis, 3 mL of cow milk was mixed with 6 mL of distilled water in a 15 mL centrifuge tube. A mixture of 10 % (v/v) trichloroacetic acid and chloroform was added, and the protein precipitate was eliminated after undergoing ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. The separation solution was then centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 15 min. The resulting supernatant was subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μM filter membrane in order to remove lipids. The treated milk samples were spiked with various concentrations of ERY, and their fluorescence response was recorded and compared to standard ERY solutions. For analysis of human serum samples, 5 mL of blood was collected from infected women using a sample tube. These blood samples were procured from the Verdi Clinical laboratory located in Sanandaj. To extract the serum, the red blood cells were separated by placing the samples in an incubator set at 37 °C for 30 min. The samples were then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min. Subsequently, the resulting serum was stored at −70 °C until further analysis. Various concentrations of ERY were introduced into the treated serum samples.

To remove suspended particles, tap water was subjected to centrifugation followed by filtration through a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm to eliminate impurities. The resulting filtrate was then used for quantitative analysis when spiked with fixed ERY concentration.

The prepared QDs and Al-MOF were characterized using various analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), UV–vis absorption, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), and fluorescence spectroscopy. The morphology and structure of the synthesized Al-MOF were initially investigated using SEM techniques. As seen in (Fig. 1A), the SEM image of Al-MOF indicated a nanosheet structure. The thickness of the nanosheet is about 100 nm. Additionally, the synthesized CdTe QDs morphology and structure are revealed by taking a TEM image, as shown in (Fig. 1B). The average size of the uniform nanoparticles is in the range of 10–25 nm, with the mean particle size of nearly 15 nm. However, the fluorescence emission spectra of Al-MOF, under varying excitation wavelengths from 290 to 370 nm, are illustrated in (Fig. 1C). As shown, the emission peak position did not change with changing the excitation wavelength, and maximum intensity was observed at 418 nm. Moreover, the 2D excitation-emission counter mappings of the Al-MOF that were created are depicted in (Fig. S1A). In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of QDs, when excited at various wavelengths between 280 and 370 nm, indicated a maximum emission intensity at 528 nm, and its emission peak's location remained unchanged, as shown in (Fig. 1D). The 2D excitation-emission counter mappings of the QDs that were created are depicted in (Fig. S1B). On the other hand, the Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) analysis revealed that the Al-MOF nanoplates exhibited a distinct spectral profile compared to NH2-BDC, as shown in (Fig. 1E). The presence of characteristic peaks at 3390 cm−1 and 3508 cm−1 indicated the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of N − H bonding, confirming the successful preservation of −NH2 after the coordination between NH2-BDC and Al3+.The wide absorption bands observed at 2300–3300 cm−1, which are associated with −COOH, exhibited a decrease in intensity for MOF compared to the proposed ligand, suggesting that NH2-BDC molecules incorporated into the structure of Al-MOF nanoplates underwent complete deprotonation (Hu et al., 2023). Furthermore, the recent peaks observed at 1062.7 cm−1 and 1010.6 cm−1 were linked to AlO bonds, indicating the presence of Al3+ bonding with O atoms in NH2-BDC. The presence of Al3+ with a small size, high charge, and strong polarization capability can react with oxygen ligands to create MOF, thereby enhancing the chemical and thermal stability of the MOF (Yue et al., 2022). Additionally, (Fig. S1C) indicates that the QDs@Al-MOF is trapped in PVA hydrogel. An examination of the FT-IR spectra in the hydrogel state revealed that the synthesized QDs@Al-MOF had absorption bands similar to those of Al-MOF and QDs. This indicates the doping of QDs and Al-MOF in hydrogel was successful. Finally, the crystallinity of Al-MOF was assessed through the utilization of X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis. The material exhibited a significant level of crystallinity, as evidenced by the presence of strong diffraction peaks in (Fig. 1F). Notably, major crystalline peaks were observed at 2θ values of 8.6°,10.5°, 15.04°,17.54°, 20.01°, and 26.3° (Kamel et al., 2021; Sharma et al., 2025).

The fluorescence spectra of excitation and emission of QDs and Al-MOF, as well as the absorption spectra of ERY and QDs were analyzed to examine the quenching mechanism of QDs, as shown in (Fig.S2A). Four key factors mainly contribute to the fluorescence quenching mechanism of carbon dots (CDs) materials: static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), the inner filter effect (IFE), and photo-induced electron transfer (PET) (Zhang et al., 2021). We analyzed the UV/vis absorption spectrum of ERY and excitation of the QDs to investigate the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism, which originates from the overlap of the absorption spectrum of the analyte and the excitation/emission spectrum of the fluorophore (Ren et al., 2019). A broad absorption peak is visible in ERY in the 225–330 nm wavelength region. The QDs spectra exhibit emission peaks at 528 nm and excitation peaks at 345 nm. The ERY absorption peak at 300 nm efficiently inhibits excitation energy, thus significantly quenching the fluorescence of the QDs. On the other hand, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of QDs overlaps with the emission peak of the Al-MOF, showing the possibility of QDs transferring energy to Al-MOF. The Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism results in a significant decrease in the QDs fluorescence through this transfer, which causes a decline in the fluorescence intensity of the donor molecules, QDs, and an enhancement in the fluorescence intensity of Al-MOF, the acceptor probe (FRET) (Fu et al., 2018). Moreover, ERY functions as an excellent ligand for Al3+, leading to the formation of an ERYAl3+ complex that amplifies the fluorescence of Al3+. As a result, the combination of ERY and Al3+ may facilitate an increase in the fluorescence emission peak at 418 nm, as indicated by the simultaneous increase in the spectrum of Al-MOF and the decline in the spectrum of QDs observed in the presence of ERY.

The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence of the QDs, Al-MOF, QDs@Al-MOF, and ERY were examined to analyze their optical characteristics. The UV–visible absorption spectra of ERY show an attractive absorption peak at 235 nm, accompanied by another peak around 300 nm, as shown in (Fig. S2B). The 235 nm peak indicates the π-π* transition of CC bonds, while the 300 nm peak may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the amine groups present on the surface of ERY (Douda et al., 2019). Additionally, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Al-MOF exhibits a peak at 325 nm, and CdTe QDs display a distinct absorption peak at 497.Moreover, the fluorescence spectra of QDs@Al-MOF were recorded with excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 350 nm in liquid form and 300 to 370 nm in hydrogel film state, as depicted in (Fig. 2A, B). The fluorescence emission intensity of Al-MOF at 418 nm increased with the increase of excitation wavelength from 280 to 330 nm but decreased at longer excitation wavelengths up to 340 nm. The most effective excitation wavelength for the generation of Al-MOF maximum emission intensity at 418 nm was identified as 330 nm. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of QDs decreased when the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 370 nm, and 330 nm was chosen for better investigations of the interaction between QDs and Al-MOF. Furthermore, (Fig. 2C) demonstrates the 2D excitation-emission counter mappings for the QDs@Al-MOF. Moreover, we investigated the fluorescence emission spectra of QDs, Al-MOF, and QDs@Al-MOF for both states. When the excitation wavelength was set at 330 nm, the QDs@Al-MOF nanocomposites emitted two different peaks, one at 418 nm and another at 528 nm. This observation provides further evidence that the preparation of QDs@Al-MOF nanocomposites was successful. However, it is important to notice that the fluorescence intensity and peak position of Al-MOF and QDs remained consistent before and after the mixing operation, as shown in (Fig. 2D, E). This suggests that the two nanocomposites preserve their respective optical properties without affecting one another. The significant difference between the two emission peaks significantly improved the visual identification of ERY.

We characterized the hydrogel film in various states, including PVA, QDs@Al-MOF-PVA, and ERY-QDs@Al-MOF-PVA, via images. We captured these images under visible and UV light exposure. The PVA hydrogel film shows a white color under visible light. However, it changes to yellow-brown when ERY and QDs@Al-MOF are entrapped in hydrogel film. Conversely, under UV light, the PVA hydrogel film color shifts to gray, while the QDs@Al-MOFs-PVA and ERY-QDs@Al-MOF-PVA hydrogel films display a green color, as depicted in (Fig. 2F). Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2F, the proposed hydrogel films are transparent and flexible. In addition, the prepared films maintain their elasticity even after two months of storage under standard laboratory settings, which indicates the applicability of the proposed films as a sensing platform for a long time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of ERY in liquid and hydrogel state with QDs@Al-MOF as a probe
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions mentioned previously, with the increasing ERY concentration, Al-MOF fluorescence intensity at 418 nm increased gradually. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity of QDs at 528 nm decreased continuously. These results are shown in (Fig. 3A and B) for both liquid and hydrogel film states. In a liquid condition, the ratio I418/I528 and the ERY concentration exhibited a linear correlation between 24.8 and 242.6 nM, as shown in (Fig. 3C). The linear regression equation for this association is I418/I528 = 0.0061 [ERY] + 0.5937 with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9902. The value of 2.16 nM was found to be the detection limit. Additionally, as shown in (Fig. 3D), the produced QDs@Al-MOF were also used for ERY detection in the hydrogel film condition, showing a linear range of (26 to 728 pM). However, the linear regression equation for this relationship, I418/I535 = 0.0034 [ERY] + 0.3878, has a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9906. The detection limit in this case was found to be 2.42 pM (S/N = 3). The QDs in the hydrogel film state showed much more enhanced stability compared with the liquid state. Additionally, the stability of the hydrogel film is confirmed through its sustained properties even after 60 h of storage in typical laboratory conditions, as depicted in (Fig. 3E and Fig. S3A). Moreover, the data collected from the experiment indicate a consistent fluorescence intensity ratio in the liquid state, ranging from 0 to 35 min, as shown in (Fig. 3F and Fig. S3B). This also confirmed that the stability of the hydrogel film state surpasses that of the liquid state. Moreover, the proposed sensor exhibited high sensitivity (500-fold) and selectivity, effectively minimizing various interferences. When compared to other published analytical techniques for ERY detection, as presented in Table 1 , QDs@Al-MOF demonstrated a low detection limit and a wide range of detection capabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone application used to identify and visualizing ERY
ce:section.ce:para: Visual detection techniques that depend on the human eye do not allow on-site quantitative measurement of ERY. We utilized a smartphone and a 365 nm UV lamp as a portable sensor platform to overcome these limitations. A smartphone can recognize the fluorescent color and RGB values as shown in (Fig. 4A). The probe of QDs@Al-MOF gradually changed from yellow to green under a 365 nm UV lamp as the ERY concentration increased. The RGB ratio was determined by the color detector application, utilizing the red (R), green (G), and blue (B) channels. A significant linear relationship between G/mean(R + G + B) and ERY concentration was observed up to a concentration of 242.6 nM, as illustrated in (Fig. 4B). With a corresponding linear equation of G/mean(R + G + B) = 0.0017 [ERY] + 1.1228 (R2 = 0.988), the lowest detectable concentration was 3.88 nM. This suggests that there is significant potential for smartphone-assisted sensing technology in the real-time quantitative detection of ERY.

In order to examine the unique selectivity of the QDs@Al-MOF sensor towards ERY, several antibiotic molecules, including metronidazole, penicillin, ceftazidime, clindamycin, ciprofloxacin, amoxicillin, and sulfamethoxazole, were selected and analyzed. For the liquid and hydrogel states, these substances were evaluated at concentrations of 500 nM and 0.5 nM, respectively. For both techniques, the ERY concentration was maintained at 50 nM and 50 pM, respectively. In this study, the QDs@Al-MOF suspension was mixed with representative interfering substances, and the resulting fluorescence spectra were recorded. This experiment was depicted in (Fig. 4C and B). Out of all the tested interfering substances, only ERY displayed a significant fluorescence response to the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on QDs@Al-MOF, while the other components displayed minimal fluorescence response. These findings suggest that the QDs@Al-MOF system possesses exceptional selectivity for the detection of ERY. Additionally, the hydrogel state is more selective than the liquid state. The enhanced structural rigidity of the hydrogel film, combined with increased availability of active sites, contributes to the increased sensitivity observed in solid state. Nevertheless, the selectivity of ERY is superior to that of other antibiotics, owing to the absorption properties of ERY that coincide with the excitation and emission spectra of fluorophores. Thus, using hydrogel as a sensor platform improves both selectivity and sensitivity.

Human serum, cow milk, and water were spiked with different amounts of the analyte ERY in both liquid and hydrogel phases to evaluate the applicability of the proposed assay for ERY analysis in real samples. The quantitative analysis of ERY in these samples involved the addition of different concentrations of ERY (24.8 nM, 147.1 nM, and 242.6 nM) in liquid form and (26 pM, 260 pM, and 728 pM) in the form of a hydrogel film to determine the recovery rates. The obtained results, as presented in Table 2 , indicate that the recovery rates of ERY ranged from 96.1 % to 102.8 % in liquid form and 96.1 % to 101.3 % in the hydrogel film state for all actual samples. These results provide strong support for the practical application of the suggested ratiometric fluorescence QDs@Al-MOF sensor in accurately detecting ERY in samples of cow milk, tap water, and human serum.

In conclusion, a novel, sensitive, and rapid method for ERY detection was successfully developed by integrating a portable smartphone with a ratiometric fluorescence sensor in both states. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was fabricated using Al-MOF nanosheets coupled with QDs for the visual assay of ERY via the IFE and FRET mechanisms. The ratiometric fluorometry approach exhibited high sensitivity, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.16 nM in liquid form and 2.42 pM in hydrogel film form for ERY detection. Notably, when comparing the hydrogel film to conventional liquid-based sensors, the LOD (500-fold) was lower. Upon gradual addition of ERY, the colorimetric fluorescent probe solution underwent a visible color change from light yellow to green, enabling the naked-eye detection of ERY. The colorimetric method demonstrated an LOD of 3.88 nM at a concentration range of up to 250 nM. Furthermore, a smartphone was utilized as an instrument for visual analysis of ERY. Consequently, the probe holds significant assurance for practical applications in the detection of ERY. This study also serves as a motivation for the development of innovative dual-mode portable devices and optical sensors based on QDs@Al-MOF. The biosensor offers advantages such as simplicity, affordability, and minimal interference, rendering it a valuable tool for the quantitative analysis of ERY in biological liquids like serum samples and food products such as milk and water. Compared with previous studies, our results also presented a favorable examination of the ERY with a large detection range, and a comparable detection limit was achieved. The hydrogel film, as an integrated dual-mode sensing platform, was promised for the detection of other analytes using a smartphone-based visual detection assay.

Kurdistan Fakhraldin Azeez: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Abdollah Salimi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Rahman Hallaj: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that there are no competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number P-S-28-12-2022) and Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.143972.
